title: Corydalis cucullaria
taxonomy: Corydalis cucullaria is classified in the family Papaveraceae, which is a group of flowering plants commonly known as poppies. The genus Corydalis is further divided into several species, each with unique characteristics and habitats. This classification is based on the plant's morphology, including its leaf shape, flower structure, and seed capsule. Within the Papaveraceae family, Corydalis cucullaria is part of the tribe Corydaleae, which includes other species of Corydalis. The exact classification of Corydalis cucullaria may vary depending on the source and taxonomic system used.
uses: Corydalis cucullaria, also known as the squirrel-corn or squirrel corn, has been utilized in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits. The plant is thought to have analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties, which may contribute to its use in treating pain and inflammation. It has been traditionally used to treat various ailments, including arthritis, gout, and other musculoskeletal issues. Additionally, Corydalis cucullaria may have neuroprotective effects, potentially making it useful in the management of neurological disorders. More research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Corydalis cucullaria as a medicinal agent.

constituents: Corydalis cucullaria, also known as the squirrel-corn or squirrel corn, contains a variety of active constituents, including alkaloids such as protopine, allocryptopine, and isocryptopine. parts: Corydalis cucullaria, commonly known as squirrel corn, is a perennial plant with medicinal properties. intro: Corydalis cucullaria, also known as squirrel corn or wild snuff, is a perennial plant native to eastern North America, belonging to the poppy family (Papaveraceae) and characterized by its distinctive, yellow to orange flowers. Traditionally, Corydalis cucullaria has been used in herbal medicine to treat various ailments, including anxiety, insomnia, and pain, due to its sedative, analgesic, and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents of this plant include alkaloids such as corydaline, corynoline, and protopine, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. Corydalis cucullaria is commonly prepared as a tincture, tea, or dried extract, and is often used in combination with other herbs to enhance its therapeutic benefits.
